answers.value:
I have been diagnosed with generalized anxiety disorder, which can become significantly worse in formal or adversarial settings. As a result, I sometimes have difficulty processing information quickly when I feel under pressure, especially over the phone or in meetings with multiple people speaking. To help me participate fully, I request the following accommodations:

1. Written Communication: Whenever possible, I prefer to receive important information, instructions, and questions in writing (email or letter) so that I have time to read, re-read, and process what is being asked before responding. I can then respond in writing or schedule a call if needed.

2. Extra Time During Interviews/Calls: If there are any interviews, intake calls, or conferences, I would appreciate being allowed extra time to answer questions, with occasional short breaks if the conversation is lengthy (for example, a 5–10 minute break after every 45–60 minutes of discussion).

3. Option to Have Support Person Present: During any formal interview or conference related to this complaint, I would like the option to have a trusted support person (such as my friend Elena or my sister) present with me, either in person or by phone/video, to help me stay calm, take notes, and remind me of details I may forget due to anxiety.

4. Clear, Step-by-Step Explanations: When possible, I benefit from clear, step-by-step explanations of processes (for example, what will happen next after each stage of the investigation, deadlines, and what is expected from me) rather than general overviews. This helps reduce anxiety and confusion.

These accommodations do not prevent me from participating; they simply help me manage my anxiety and ensure that I can provide accurate, complete information throughout the process.

answers.value:
Because English is my second language, I sometimes struggle with complex legal or technical vocabulary, especially when spoken quickly. I am generally comfortable in English for everyday matters, but when it comes to legal rights, deadlines, or anything that could affect my employment or benefits, I feel much more secure if I can either:

- Have access to a Spanish interpreter for key meetings or calls; and/or
- Receive important documents in both English and Spanish so I can compare and make sure I understand.

I also work a full-time schedule (Monday–Friday, 8:30 a.m. to 5:00 p.m. Mountain Time). If possible, I would appreciate scheduling calls either early in the morning (before 8:30), during my lunch break (usually 12:00–12:45), or after 5:00 p.m., or communicating by email so I can respond outside of my work hours without jeopardizing my current job. I do not want my current employer to know about this complaint unless it becomes absolutely necessary as part of the process.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: To Settle This Complaint I Would Accept
answers.value:
I am filing this complaint because I believe I was subjected to discrimination and retaliation at my former workplace, a mid-sized logistics company in Denver, after I reported repeated comments and treatment that targeted my national origin (Mexican-American) and accent. The situation escalated over several months, and ultimately I feel that I was pushed out of my position through unfair discipline and a hostile work environment.

To settle this complaint, I would accept a resolution that addresses both the harm I have already suffered and helps prevent similar situations from happening to others. Specifically, I would consider the complaint resolved if the following conditions are met:

1. Monetary Compensation:
   - Back Pay: Payment for the wages and benefits I lost from the time my hours were significantly reduced in March 2025 until my eventual resignation in July 2025. This should include the difference between what I actually earned and what I would have earned if my schedule and position had remained unchanged, including any overtime I was regularly working before the retaliation started.
   - Front Pay or Transitional Support: A reasonable amount of front pay or a lump-sum payment to cover the period I was unemployed or underemployed while looking for a new job (approximately 3–4 months). During that time, I had to dip into my small savings and borrow money from my sister to cover rent and utilities, so compensation that helps me stabilize my finances again is very important.
   - Compensation for Emotional Distress: A separate monetary amount to recognize the emotional harm and stress I experienced, including anxiety, loss of sleep, and the impact on my self-esteem and professional reputation. I began seeing a therapist in May 2025 because of panic attacks related to going into work, and I am still in therapy now. I am not seeking an unrealistic amount, but I do believe there should be meaningful recognition of the emotional and psychological impact this situation had on me.

2. Correction of Employment Records:
   - Removal or Correction of Negative Write-Ups: I would like the employer to remove or formally correct any unfair disciplinary write-ups, performance warnings, or notes placed in my file after I complained about the discriminatory comments. These documents falsely portray me as having attendance problems and being “not a team player,” which is not consistent with my prior good performance reviews. Having these records corrected is important so that if future employers contact them, they do not receive a distorted, retaliatory picture of my work history.
   - Neutral Reference: I want a written agreement that the employer will provide only a neutral reference if contacted by prospective employers—confirming only my dates of employment, job title(s), and, if necessary, final rate of pay—without negative or retaliatory commentary.

3. Policy and Training Changes at the Company:
   - Anti-Discrimination and Anti-Retaliation Training: I would like the company to provide a meaningful, in-person or live virtual training (not just a quick online module) for managers and supervisors in my former department on discrimination, harassment based on national origin and accent, and retaliation. This training should specifically address:
     * That mocking an employee’s accent or making comments like “We’re in America, speak English clearly” can be discriminatory and create a hostile environment.
     * That employees who bring forward complaints in good faith must not be punished through reduced hours, unfavorable shifts, or unfair discipline.
   - Clear Complaint Procedure: I would like the company to review and, if necessary, revise its internal complaint procedure so that employees understand clearly how to report discrimination safely, what will happen after they report, and how investigations will be documented. Ideally, this should include an option to report to someone other than the direct supervisor, since in my case, my direct supervisor was part of the problem.

4. Written Acknowledgment (Non-Confidential if Possible):
   - While I do not expect the company to admit legal liability, I would like some form of written acknowledgment that the situation was handled poorly and that steps are being taken to improve workplace culture and training. Even a carefully worded statement that recognizes that I raised legitimate concerns and that the company is committed to treating employees fairly would help me feel that my experience led to some positive change.

5. No Gag Order on My Experience:
   - I would prefer that any settlement agreement not contain overly broad confidentiality or non-disparagement clauses that would prevent me from talking about my personal experience with close family, my therapist, or potential employers (in a factual way). I understand there may be some reasonable confidentiality terms, but I do not want to feel legally silenced about what happened to me.

Overall, I am open to discussing specific dollar amounts and details through mediation or conciliation, as long as the final outcome fairly reflects the financial losses I suffered, the emotional and psychological harm, and includes some concrete steps by the employer to address discrimination and retaliation going forward. My main goals are to regain financial stability, clear my professional name, and know that my complaint helped make the workplace safer and more respectful for others.

answers.value: Former coworker on the same logistics team; we worked the same shift for about 2.5 years and reported to the same supervisor.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Witness 1 What Did This Person Witness
answers.value:
Daniel was present for several key incidents and can speak both to my overall work performance and to the change in how I was treated after I raised concerns.

Specific things Daniel witnessed include:

1. Discriminatory Comments About My Accent and Background:
   - On at least two occasions in February and early March 2025, Daniel was in the small break room when our supervisor, Mark Thompson, made comments about my accent. For example, after I finished a phone call with a vendor, Mark said, in front of Daniel and another coworker, “Maria, you need to slow down and speak more ‘American.’ They probably can’t understand you half the time.” Daniel later told me privately that he thought the comment was inappropriate and that Mark said it in a mocking tone, kind of exaggerating my pronunciation.
   - Daniel also overheard Mark say things like, “We’re not in Mexico, we don’t do things that way here,” when I suggested a different way of organizing the inventory area. Daniel was close enough to hear Mark’s exact words and saw my reaction, as well as how uncomfortable I became afterward.

2. My Work Performance and Reliability:
   - Daniel worked closely with me for over two years and can confirm that before I complained, I was considered reliable, often volunteered for overtime, and helped train new hires. He observed that I consistently arrived on time, completed my tasks, and often stayed late when shipments were delayed. This is important because, after I complained, the company began issuing me write-ups for supposedly being late or “not following instructions,” which Daniel knows is inconsistent with my actual behavior and prior record.

3. Change in Schedule and Treatment After My Complaint:
   - After I reported the discriminatory comments to HR in mid-March 2025, Daniel noticed that my schedule was suddenly changed from a stable Monday–Friday daytime shift to a mix of late evening and weekend shifts, even though I had seniority over some of the newer employees who kept their preferred schedules.
   - Daniel told me he thought it was strange that I went from having regular overtime to having my hours cut back to almost exactly 32 hours per week. He can describe how, before the complaint, I was usually scheduled around 40–45 hours, and how this changed afterward, even when the workload stayed high.

4. Retaliatory Write-Ups and Hostile Atmosphere:
   - Daniel was present on one occasion when Mark loudly reprimanded me on the warehouse floor for supposedly mislabeling a shipment. Daniel later checked the paperwork and realized the error had originated in the system before I handled it. He can attest that Mark raised his voice and criticized me in front of others, which he did not usually do with other employees for similar mistakes.
   - Daniel also saw me being excluded from team meetings that I used to attend. For example, he mentioned a scheduling meeting in April 2025 where my name was not included on the invite, even though my role had not changed. He only realized this when I asked him about changes to the weekend coverage and he was surprised I had not been in the meeting.

Overall, Daniel can provide detailed, first-hand information about the workplace environment, the pattern of comments that targeted my accent and national origin, the shift in my schedule and hours after I complained, and the difference between how I was treated compared to other employees who did not speak with an accent or who did not raise complaints.

answers.value: HR representative at my former employer; she conducted the initial meeting when I reported discrimination and handled some of the follow-up communication.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Witness 2 What Did This Person Witness
answers.value:
Patricia is the HR generalist who met with me when I first reported the discrimination and retaliation, so she can confirm that I raised my concerns in a timely and detailed manner, and that I tried to resolve the situation internally before filing this external complaint.

Specific things Patricia witnessed or has direct knowledge of include:

1. My Initial Complaint Meeting:
   - In mid-March 2025 (I believe around March 14, 2025), I scheduled a meeting with Patricia in her office. During that meeting, I described several incidents involving my supervisor, Mark Thompson, including:
     * His comments about my accent, such as telling me to speak more “American” and mocking the way I said certain words.
     * The comment about “We’re not in Mexico” after I suggested an organizational change.
     * The way he would sometimes imitate my accent when repeating my instructions to other employees.
   - Patricia took notes during this meeting and asked follow-up questions. She can confirm that I was visibly upset and that I specifically used the words “discrimination” and “hostile environment” when describing how the comments made me feel.

2. My Report of Retaliation:
   - I also told Patricia that, shortly after I had informally asked Mark to stop making comments about my accent, my schedule started to change, and I was being assigned less favorable shifts. At that time, the changes were just beginning, but I expressed that I was worried it was retaliation for speaking up.
   - Patricia said she would “look into it” and talk to Mark and possibly his manager. She also told me that the company did not tolerate discrimination or retaliation and that she would follow up with me once she had more information.

3. HR’s Handling of the Investigation (or Lack Thereof):
   - Patricia sent me a follow-up email about a week later stating that she had spoken with Mark and that he “did not recall” making any discriminatory comments, but that he agreed to be more mindful of his tone. She told me in that email that the matter was considered “addressed” but encouraged me to come back if anything else happened.
   - In a later conversation (around late April 2025), I informed Patricia that my hours had been cut and that I had received a written warning for supposed attendance issues that I believed were exaggerated or inaccurate. Patricia acknowledged my concerns but said that scheduling and performance issues were “management decisions” and that HR could not “micro-manage” every choice.
   - Patricia can confirm the timeline of when I brought up retaliation and what, if anything, HR did in response. She may also have access to emails, notes, or internal documents related to my complaint and the follow-up.

4. Documentation and Records:
   - Patricia is likely familiar with my personnel file, including my earlier positive performance reviews and the sudden appearance of multiple write-ups in the months after my complaint. While she may not have witnessed every incident personally, she can confirm the sequence of documents and the fact that my record changed significantly after I reported discrimination.
   - She can also confirm that I did not have a history of serious disciplinary issues before March 2025 and that I had been considered a reliable employee.

5. My Attempts to Resolve the Situation Internally:
   - Patricia can verify that I made good-faith efforts to resolve the issues within the company. I met with her more than once, responded to her emails, and followed the internal complaint process as it was explained to me.
   - She can also confirm that, during our last conversation before I resigned, I told her I felt I was being pushed out and that I was afraid my hours and reputation were being damaged because I spoke up about discrimination.

Overall, while Patricia may not have personally seen every discriminatory comment, she is a key witness to the fact that I reported the behavior, that HR was made aware of possible discrimination and retaliation, and that the company’s response was minimal and did not stop the negative treatment from continuing. Her testimony and records can help establish the timeline and show that my complaint was not an afterthought but something I raised while I was still employed and actively trying to fix the situation.
